display_name: A NAT translation entry existing does not mean traffic is being NAT'd — check order of operations
context: Troubleshooting traffic not reaching its destination despite a NAT translation appearing in the translation table
observation: The NAT translation table shows the expected mapping (inside local/global or outside local/global), but traffic still does not flow end-to-end
naive_interpretation: NAT is working correctly because the translation exists, so the problem must be elsewhere in the path (routing, ACL, or the far end)
correct_interpretation: A static NAT translation entry exists in the table regardless of whether traffic matches it. A dynamic entry may have been created by earlier traffic and is now stale. The presence of a translation does NOT confirm that current traffic is being translated. NAT has a specific order of operations relative to routing and ACLs that differs by direction (inside-to-outside vs outside-to-inside) and by platform. On IOS/IOS-XE: inside-to-outside traffic is routed THEN NAT'd; outside-to-inside traffic is NAT'd THEN routed. If routing sends traffic out the wrong interface before NAT can translate it, or if an ACL on the inside interface drops the pre-NAT (original) source, the translation exists but is never applied. Check interface NAT designations (ip nat inside/outside), routing for BOTH pre-NAT and post-NAT addresses, and ACL match criteria relative to NAT order.
why: Static NAT entries are configured, not flow-created — they exist in the table whether or not any traffic matches them
Dynamic NAT entries have a timeout and may represent a previous flow that has since stopped; the entry lingers but no active flow uses it

NAT order of operations on IOS: inside-to-outside = route lookup, then translate source; outside-to-inside = translate destination, then route lookup — ACLs and routing see different addresses depending on direction
NAT interface designation (ip nat inside/outside) determines which translations apply on which interfaces; a missing or wrong designation silently skips NAT

confirmation_commands.purpose: View current translations and check hit counts — a translation with zero or stale hits is not actively translating traffic
Check total hits/misses and which interfaces are designated inside/outside to confirm NAT is positioned correctly
Verify translation entries and their hit counts on NX-OS
Verify routing for both pre-NAT and post-NAT addresses to ensure traffic reaches the correct NAT interface

traps: Declaring NAT healthy because a translation exists in the table, then spending hours debugging routing or ACLs that are actually correct for the post-NAT address
Not checking whether the interfaces have the correct ip nat inside/outside designation — traffic on an undesignated interface bypasses NAT entirely
Writing ACLs matching post-NAT addresses on an interface where NAT hasn't been applied yet (wrong point in the order of operations)
Forgetting that dynamic NAT entries time out — a translation you saw 5 minutes ago may be gone, and a new flow may be failing because the NAT pool is exhausted
Confusing static NAT (always present, bidirectional) with dynamic NAT (flow-created, directional, subject to pool/overload limits)

sources.title: RFC 3022 — Traditional IP Network Address Translator (Traditional NAT)
